history_summary: Initial Task: make a very good e commerce webpage

Claude responded: Project Vision: Meridian Pour — Specialty Coffee Equipment E-Commerce
Recalled memory
Recalled memory
Project Vision: Meridian Pour — Specialty Coffee Equipment E-Commerce
1. Core Concept & Vibe
Meridian Pour is a direct-to-consumer e-commerce site for precision coffee brewing equipment — pour-over drippers, grinders, scales, and kettles aimed at serious home baristas and coffee professionals. The audience is technically-minded enthusiasts who care about engineering, materials, and repeatable results — think the same crowd that reads spec sheets for mechanical keyboards or audio gear.
The vibe is precision manufacturing meets everyday ritual: clean, confident, unfussy. Not warm and rustic (that's the coffee-shop cliché to avoid) — instead it should feel closer to a well-made tool catalog or a hardware startup's site. Calm, high-contrast, information-dense where it matters, spacious where it doesn't. The product should feel like it was engineered, not brewed.
Design system pairing: Pair 1 (Clean Modern / shadcn-style) — Inter across display and body, JetBrains Mono for technical specs (weights, flow rates, temperatures). Sharp, confident, systematic — matches the "engineered tool" positioning.
2. Key Features & User Flows
Homepage — hero introducing the brand's design philosophy, a featured "system" (dripper + kettle + scale bundle), category entry points (Drippers, Grinders, Scales, Kettles, Accessories), and a short editorial section on brewing precision.
Shop/Category page — grid of products with filtering by category, price range, and material (ceramic, stainless, glass); sort by price/newest/best-selling.
Product detail page — hero product imagery, technical spec table (dimensions, capacity, materials, flow rate where relevant), a "compatible with" cross-sell module (e.g., filters that pair with a dripper), quantity selector, add-to-cart.
Cart drawer — slide-out cart with line-item quantity edit, subtotal, and a persistent "free shipping over $X" progress indicator.
Checkout flow — single-page checkout: shipping info → payment → order review, with an order summary sidebar that stays visible throughout.
Brewing Guides page — lightweight editorial/content hub linking specific guides to specific products (reinforces the technical-precision positioning without needing full CMS depth).
Account/Order confirmation — simple post-purchase confirmation screen with order number and summary.
3. Design Direction
Light background as the primary canvas, with a single confident accent color used sparingly (CTAs, active states, key data points) — no gradients, no multi-color palettes.
Product photography treated as the hero: large, well-lit, consistent framing across the catalog — if using placeholder imagery, favor clean studio-style product shots over lifestyle stock photos.
Technical specs should be presented with monospace typography and tabular alignment — make the precision tangible, not just implied.
Micro-interactions on hover/add-to-cart should feel snappy and mechanical (short, purposeful transitions) — nothing bouncy or playful.
Generous whitespace on marketing sections, tighter and denser on shop/spec sections — the contrast between the two rhythms should itself communicate "considered design."
Sharp-to-moderate border radius personality (pick one and hold it everywhere — buttons, cards, inputs) reinforcing the engineered, non-organic feel.
4. Data Needs
Mock catalog of ~16–20 products across 5 categories (Drippers, Grinders, Scales, Kettles, Accessories), each with name, price, short description, category, material, 2–3 technical spec fields, and stock status.
3–4 "system" bundles pairing complementary products together.
A handful of brewing guide entries (title, short summary, associated product reference) for the Guides page.
Basic customer/order shape for cart and checkout flow (no real payment processing needed — mock/simulated confirmation is sufficient).
Created and edited memory
Created and edited memory
That's the vision — ready for Antigravity to build from.

